1: Darius issues the decree for an archive search. The Persian imperial archive system — spanning the empire’s multiple capitals and storage facilities — was one of the most sophisticated record-keeping operations of the ancient world. The search for Cyrus’s decree involves looking not just at Babylon (where Cyrus reigned) but at multiple possible locations where official records were kept.
2: The decree is found at Ecbatana (modern Hamadan in western Iran), the ancient Median capital that served as the Persian summer residence. Cyrus had been at Ecbatana when he issued the decree in 538/537 BCE; official records were kept at the location where the decree was issued. The scroll (gᵉlîlāh) format — a leather or papyrus roll — is the standard archival medium for official Persian correspondence.
3: The Cyrus decree as found: “Let the house be rebuilt, the place where they offer sacrifices, and let its foundations be maintained.” The archival version differs slightly from the version cited by the returning exiles in Ezra 1:2-4 — the archival version focuses on dimensions and construction specifications (v3-4) while Ezra 1:2-4 is a proclamation version for public dissemination. Both are authentic; they represent different forms of the same decree for different audiences.
4: Specifications: three courses of large stones and one course of timber, with all expenses paid from the royal treasury. The alternating stone-and-timber construction technique is confirmed by archaeological evidence from the Persian period. The royal treasury funding converts the temple rebuilding from a private religious project into an officially sponsored imperial project — changing its legal status entirely.
5: Furthermore, the gold and silver vessels that Nebuchadnezzar took from Jerusalem are to be returned to the temple in Jerusalem. The explicit mention of the vessels’ return confirms the correspondence between the oral tradition of the returned exiles (Ezra 5:14-15) and the written archive: both independently attest the vessels’ transfer to Sheshbazzar. The legal chain is established.
6: Darius issues his own decree to Tattenai: “Stay away from there. Do not interfere with the work on God’s house.” The complete reversal of Artaxerxes’ halt (Ezra 4:21-23): Tattenai’s honest investigation has produced the opposite of what Rehum’s hostile accusation achieved. The same Persian administrative system that was weaponized against the builders is now turned to their protection.
7: “Let the governor of the Jews and the Jewish elders rebuild God’s house where it was.” Darius grants explicit provincial authority to the Jewish leadership for the building project. The title “governor of the Jews” (paḥat Yᵉhûḏāyēʾ) is an official administrative designation that recognizes Jewish self-governance within the Persian satrapal system.
8: Darius goes further: “I hereby order what you are to do for these Jewish elders as they build God’s house: from the royal revenue of Trans-Euphrates the costs are to be given to these men.” Not only does Darius permit the building; he funds it from the provincial tax revenues. The opponents who sought to deny the builders imperial support have inadvertently produced a decision that gives them imperial financing.
9: Darius orders provision of animals for sacrifice — young bulls, rams, lambs for burnt offerings; wheat, salt, wine, and olive oil — daily as needed, without hindrance. The detail of “salt without limit” (mᵉlaḥ lāʾ ḥāšᵃḥ) is significant: salt was used extensively in sacrifice (Leviticus 2:13; Numbers 18:19; Mark 9:49) and was a government-controlled commodity in the ancient world.
10: “So that they may offer acceptable sacrifices to the God of heaven and pray for the welfare of the king and his sons.” Darius’s motivation is explicitly pragmatic piety: he funds Jewish sacrifices to receive Jewish intercessory prayer. The same logic that Cyrus employed (Ezra 1:2: YHWH charged me) is here expressed more clearly as an exchange: imperial support for temple worship, in return for divine favor on the empire. Persian royal theology accommodated supporting multiple divine cults.
11: The enforcement clause: anyone who defies this order shall have a beam pulled from his house, impaled on it, and his house made a dunghill (nᵉwalû). The grotesque punishment — being impaled on timber from one’s own house, which is then destroyed — is darkly satirical: Rehum and Shimshai tried to stop the building of YHWH’s house; anyone who interferes with it will have their own house become an instrument of their execution.
12: “May the God who has caused his name to dwell there overthrow any king or people who tries to alter this or to destroy this house of God in Jerusalem.” The divine name theology (šᵉkān šᵉmēhw tammāh) — the presence of YHWH’s name dwelling in the temple — appears in an Aramaic imperial decree. Darius invokes YHWH’s self-interest in the temple’s preservation as the ultimate guarantee of the decree: YHWH himself will punish violators. This is Solomon’s temple-dedication theology (1 Kings 8:16-20) pressed into service as Persian imperial law.
13: Tattenai, Shetharboznai, and their associates carry out the decree exactly. The honest administrator’s response to royal decision: complete and immediate compliance. The same zeal that had led Rehum and Shimshai to “immediately” force the halt (Ezra 4:23) now leads Tattenai to immediately implement the resumption. Administrative efficiency works in both directions.
14: The elders build and prosper (wᵉṣālaḥ, succeeded) under the prophetic encouragement of Haggai and Zechariah. The building is completed “by the command of the God of Israel and by decree of Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes.” The combination of divine command and imperial decree is the Chronicler’s understanding of post-exilic history: YHWH’s purposes are implemented through successive Persian kings, each of whom served the divine will without knowing it as fully as the Chronicler does.
15: The house is completed on the 3rd of Adar in the 6th year of Darius = March 12, 515 BCE. After the laying of the foundation in 536 BCE (Ezra 3:10), the sixteen-year halt (Ezra 4:24), and the four-year second phase of construction (520-515 BCE), the second temple is finally complete. The completion date — twenty-one years after the return from exile — marks the fulfillment of the first phase of the post-exilic mission.
16: The dedication celebration — Israelites, priests, Levites, and rest of the returned exiles celebrate with joy. The title “returned exiles” (bᵉnê gālûtāʾ, sons of the exile) becomes the technical designation for the community throughout Ezra-Nehemiah: they are defined by their exile experience and their return, not merely by their tribal membership.
17: Dedication offerings: 100 bulls, 200 rams, 400 lambs, 12 goats for the sin offering. The scale is a fraction of Solomon’s dedication (22,000 oxen, 120,000 sheep; 2 Chronicles 7:5) — a visible marker of the second temple’s humbler glory (cf. Haggai 2:3: “how does it look to you now? Does it not seem to you like nothing?”). But the 12 goats — one for each tribe of Israel — maintain the all-Israel symbolism even though only a portion of Israel was present. The symbolic 12 transcends the actual number.
18: “They appointed the priests to their divisions and the Levites to their courses for the worship of God in Jerusalem, as it is written in the book of Moses.” The restoration of Mosaic liturgical order — priests and Levites in their Davidic divisions (1 Chr 23-26) according to the written Torah — is the organizational counterpart to the building’s physical completion. The temple is complete when both the structure and the worship community are properly ordered.
19: The returned exiles observe the Passover on the 14th of the first month (Nisan 14). This is the first Passover in the covenant land since the exile — the feast that commemorates redemption from Egypt is now celebrated by a community that has experienced its own exodus from Babylonian captivity. The calendar correspondence is intentional: the feast of the first exodus is the appropriate occasion for commemorating the second.
20: The priests and Levites had all purified themselves as a group — “they were all ritually clean.” The comprehensive purity of the temple personnel stands in contrast to the mixed-purity situation at Hezekiah’s Passover (2 Chronicles 30:17-20), where many came without proper purification. The completeness of the post-exilic purification reflects the seriousness with which the returned community took covenant observance after the exile’s lesson.
21: The Passover is eaten by the returned exiles along with “all who had separated themselves from the uncleanness of the peoples of the land to seek the LORD, the God of Israel.” The Passover extends beyond the strictly genetic Jewish community to include any who have committed to covenant fidelity. The boundary marker is not ethnicity but practice: separation from idolatry plus orientation toward YHWH. This anticipates the later conversion discussions in Nehemiah and is consistent with the OT’s broader treatment of the gēr (sojourner) who keeps Passover (Exodus 12:48-49).
22: “The LORD had filled them with joy and had turned the heart of the king of Assyria to them.” The identification of the Persian king as “king of Assyria” is the Chronicler’s theological shorthand: Persia has inherited the role of the dominant empire (Assyria = the generic imperial power that rules the region); the same God who could turn the heart of Assyrian kings controls Persian kings. The joy of the feast is attributed directly to YHWH: the empire funded the feast; YHWH filled the feast with joy.
